data.title: Parnassus Value Select ETF Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Facts

Ticker PRVS
Total Expense Ratio 1 0.59%
Inception Date 12/11/2024
Benchmark Russell 1000 Value Index
Asset Class U.S. large cap value
Objective Capital appreciation

The strategy seeks to achieve strong long-term returns through a high-conviction, highly concentrated portfolio of approximately 25 undervalued U.S. large-cap stocks that are poised to rise but are temporarily out of favor relative to their history or peers.

Key Takeaways

  • Parnassus Value Select ETF (NAV)(PRVS) returned -1.45% (NAV) for the quarter, underperforming the Russell 1000 Value Index's 2.10%.
  • The portfolio's performance was primarily impacted by our lack of exposure to the Energy sector, along with our holdings in the Health Care and Financials sectors. Performance was helped by our holdings in the Communication Services, Industrials and Information Technology sectors.
  • Despite the increased volatility in the first quarter, we continue to maintain a quality-oriented portfolio with balanced exposure across traditional value and secular themes.
  • Markets have repeatedly rewarded investors who maintained a disciplined approach and long-term orientation through periods of uncertainty. We believe our quality-anchored portfolio will prove resilient through the current volatility.

Market Review

The Russell 1000 Value Index added to 2025's strong return with a modest gain in the first quarter, significantly outperforming its growth peer, as geopolitical uncertainty put downward pressure on multiple expansion. Equity markets reversed course in the first quarter of 2026, as early optimism surrounding the ongoing megatrend in artificial intelligence (AI) and easier monetary policy yielded to geopolitical shocks and tighter financial conditions. While the AI megatrend remained intact, fears of AI disruption caused sharp sell-offs among asset-light businesses such as software. The Federal Reserve held interest rates steady due to persistent inflation and economic uncertainty. The escalation of the U.S.-Iran war drove oil prices sharply higher and resulted in a shift to a risk-off market sentiment, which pressured economically sensitive stocks while buoying the Energy sector. During the quarter, the best-performing sectors in the Russell 1000 Value Index were Energy, Industrials and Consumer Staples, while the Financials sector ended the quarter with sharp losses. The Communication Services, Consumer Discretionary and Health Care sectors finished modestly lower.

Performance

As of 03/31/2026 3 Mos. 1 Yr. Since 12/11/2024
Parnassus Value Select ETF - NAV -1.45 18.81 8.26
Parnassus Value Select ETF - Market Price -1.66 18.54 8.17
Russell 1000 Value 2.10 15.87 10.46

Performance data quoted represent past performance and are no guarantee of future returns. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted, and current performance information to the most recent month end is available on the Parnassus website (www.parnassus.com). Investment return and principal value will fluctuate, so an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original principal cost. Returns shown in the table do not reflect the deduction of taxes a shareholder may pay on fund distributions or redemption of shares. The Russell 1000 Value Index is an unmanaged index of common stocks, and it is not possible to invest directly in an index. Index figures do not take any expenses, fees or taxes into account, but mutual fund returns do. The estimated impact of individual stocks on the Fund's performance is provided by FactSet.

Quarterly Performance Review

Energy underweight impacted relative performance

The Fund returned -1.45% (NAV) for the quarter, underperforming the Russell 1000 Value Index's 2.10%. The portfolio's underperformance was largely attributed to a lack of exposure to the Energy sector, which delivered strong returns for the quarter due to rising energy prices and heightened geopolitical uncertainty rather than a structural change in fundamentals. Our Health Care and Financials holdings also detracted. Our holdings in the Communication Services, Industrials and Information Technology sectors boosted relative returns, as investors moderated expectations for growth stocks and rotated into companies with resilient and more predictable earnings.

Top Quarterly Contributors

Applied Materials (AMAT), which supplies semiconductor manufacturing equipment, benefited from higher demand from foundry customers tied to AI infrastructure build-out, as hyperscalers increased spending to support accelerating compute needs.

Deere (DE) advanced on expectations for an agricultural cycle recovery and continued adoption of precision agriculture technologies, with investors viewing the agricultural equipment business as both durable and future proofed.

Verizon Communications (VZ) shares climbed as the telecommunications company benefited from a change in strategy under a new CEO to prioritize subscriber retention, while investors shifted into more defensive stocks over the period due to market volatility.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co.'s (TSMC)(TSM) central role in AI chip production continued to bolster investor confidence and drive stock gains, as company management announced higher-than-anticipated capital expenditure guidance.

AstraZeneca (AZN), a multinational pharmaceutical and biotechnology company, outperformed as the company demonstrated strong pipeline execution, boosted by resilient demand across oncology and specialty medicines. The shift into more defensive names amid the market volatility also boosted investor sentiment.

Bottom Quarterly Contributors

Microsoft (MSFT) shares ended lower amid continued investor fears around the potential return on its elevated AI-related capital expenditures, despite the company reporting healthy financial results.

CBRE Group (CBRE) shares declined on higher financing costs for the commercial real estate sector and fears that AI could disrupt the company's traditional commercial real estate brokerage business model. We believe AI will be net additive to CBRE's moat given its vast data advantage.

S&P Global (SPGI) underperformed as uncertainty around the theoretical ease of replacing financial data providers with AI tools weighed on sentiment. We expect the company to remain an important player in its debt rating, data and risk management market segments.

Visa (V) shares fell despite management's strong 2026 earnings outlook, as litigation-related overhangs, regulatory concerns and a more cautious macro backdrop weighed on investor sentiment. Still, we believe Visa is a net beneficiary of AI, with its exposure to agentic commerce.

Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), a global provider of pharmaceutical research instruments and services, declined alongside life sciences peers as investors remained cautious on the pace of end-market recovery, despite fundamentals tracking expectations.

Security Avg. Weight (%) Total Return (%) Allocation Effect (%)
Applied Materials 4.71 33.16 1.00
Deere 3.72 21.34 0.91
Verizon Comm. 4.39 25.39 0.79
TSMC 4.60 11.45 0.37
AstraZeneca 5.45 7.74 0.35
Security Avg. Weight (%) Total Return (%) Allocation Effect (%)

Return calculations are gross of fees, time weighted and geometrically linked. Returns would be lower as a result of the deduction of fees.

Portfolio Positioning

Maintaining a quality-oriented portfolio amid heightened uncertainty

We continue to maintain a quality-oriented portfolio with balanced exposure across traditional value and secular themes. While geopolitical risks have risen in recent months, valuations are also more attractive for companies with high earnings quality. The current environment offers opportunity for value investors with a quality focus and long-term investment horizon.

The Portfolio's largest sector overweights are in Information Technology, Health Care and Communication Services. Our largest underweights are in Energy, Consumer Staples and Industrials.

Within Information Technology, our portfolio weight increased modestly as our semiconductor holdings rose during the quarter. The AI investment cycle continues to broaden to a wider set of ecosystem players, benefiting our holdings such as Applied Materials, Micron and TSMC. We remain optimistic about the adoption of AI but believe selectivity through bottom-up stock picking remains critical because opportunities and risks are highly company specific and spread across different sectors and time horizons.

During the quarter, portfolio exposures decreased modestly within Health Care and increased modestly within Communications Services on account of stock price movements. Our defensive holdings in those sectors, such as Verizon and AstraZeneca, held up well during this period of market weakness. This exemplifies our balanced approach to portfolio construction, with ample focus on resilience during periods of market volatility.

Our underweight positioning in Consumer Staples and Consumer Discretionary sectors remained broadly unchanged over the quarter. We continue to maintain diversified risk-adjusted exposure to consumer spending through payment networks and bank card issuers.

We view the relatively resilient economic data and company fundamentals as an attractive risk/reward opportunity for investors today. We continue to maintain our highest convictions in undervalued quality companies with the financial strength to withstand economic headwinds and positioned to benefit from long-term thematic tailwinds.

Outlook

Disciplined value investing through volatility and uncertainty

Value stocks continued their outperformance during the first quarter. The Russell 1000 Value Index has now significantly outperformed its growth peer over the last six months. This renewed period of market uncertainty—driven by the conflict in the Middle East, concerns about disruption from AI and volatility in interest rates—continues to favor stocks with more reasonable valuations over those embedding high market expectations. We believe our quality-anchored portfolio should prove resilient if geopolitical risks broaden into bigger headwinds for the global economy. Moreover, fundamental tailwinds underpinning long-term upside in the companies we hold in the portfolio remain intact despite near-term market volatility.

Every crisis is different in some ways and carries with it a different set of risks. However, looking back on the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, the Ukraine war in 2022, the regional banking crisis in 2023 and the tariff-driven sell-off of 2025, a consistent lesson emerges: Markets have repeatedly rewarded investors who maintained a disciplined approach and long-term orientation through periods of uncertainty. This lesson also applies to how we evaluate management teams. We invest behind leaders with demonstrated experience in navigating uncertainty and the ability to stay steadfast in driving long-term shareholder value.

As stewards of your capital, we remain vigilant about macro risks, but our fundamental, bottom-up investment process avoids undue reliance on investing around macro outcomes. In our experience, fundamental factors can far outweigh near-term macro factors in generating attractive returns. We invest with a long-term time horizon that helps us weather short-term volatility and stay focused on what matters most—owning quality businesses at attractive valuations in the pursuit of strong long-term returns.

Quarterly Portfolio Activity

N/A There were no additions or eliminations to the portfolio during the period.

Glossary

Earnings Growth is the change in a company's reported net income over a period of time.

Allocation Effect: the allocation effect in performance attribution measures how much of a portfolio's outperformance or underperformance against a benchmark comes from the manager's decision to overweight or underweight specific sectors or asset classes, rather than from picking individual stocks within them.

Important Information

PIL-916805-2026-04-14

The Russell 1000® Value Index is a market capitalization weighted index that measures the performance of those Russell 1000 companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth rates. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with relatively lower price-to-book ratios, lower I/B/E/S forecast medium term (2 year) growth and lower sales per share historical growth (5 years). The Russell 1000® Value Index is constructed to provide a comprehensive and unbiased barometer for the large cap value segment. The index is completely reconstituted annually to ensure new and growing equities are included and that the represented companies continue to reflect value characteristics.

1. As described in the Fund's current prospectus dated April 28, 2025, as Amended and Restated September 22, 2025, Parnassus Investments has contractually agreed to reduce its investment advisory fee to the extent necessary to limit total operating expenses to 0.59% of the Fund's average daily net assets. This agreement will not be terminated prior to May 1, 2026, and may be continued indefinitely by the investment adviser on a year-to-year basis.

ENVIRONMENTAL, SOCIAL AND GOVERNANCE (ESG) GUIDELINES: The Fund evaluates ESG factors as part of the investment decision-making process, considering a range of impacts they may have on future revenues, expenses, assets, liabilities and overall risk. The Fund also utilizes active ownership to encourage more sustainable business policies and practices and greater ESG transparency. Active ownership strategies include proxy voting, dialogue with company management and sponsorship of shareholder resolutions, and public policy advocacy. There is no guarantee that the ESG strategy will be successful.

Investing involves risk and principal loss is possible. ETFs may trade at a premium or discount to NAV. Shares of any ETF are bought and sold at market prices (not NAV) and are not individually redeemed from the Fund. Brokerage commissions will reduce returns.

data.title: Exelixis: The Double Backbone Potential Of CABOMETYX And Zanzalintinib
data.description:
Exelixis (EXEL) is not a new ticker to me; in fact, it has been in the Compounding Healthcare “Bioreactor” Growth Portfolio for years. Yet, I’ve never formally covered the company with a Seeking Alpha premium article. This wasn’t due to a lack of conviction in the company's performance or outlook. It was primarily a result of the company being so steady with CABOMETYX that the ticker didn’t always need constant analysis. However, I believe the status quo is evolving after reading a recent conference transcript that suggests Zanzalintinib could have blockbuster potential and become a second backbone TKI for Exelixis. Admittedly, I was initially concerned that zanzalintinib would essentially replace CABOMETYX and end up cannibalizing its revenue. However, a deeper analysis showed that although CABOMETYX and zanzalintinib are similar oncology drugs, it is possible that they can coexist because they can be deployed in different lines and therapy combinations. It looks as if it is possible that CABOMETYX could remain the durable cash generator, while zanzalintinib can be the next big growth driver for the 2030s. As a result, I’m looking to add EXEL to my high-priority watch list as we start to head towards the second half of 2026.

I intend to provide a brief background on Exelixis and review their recent performance. Then, I will discuss why I believe CABOMETYX and Zanzalintinib can cohabit with different roles in the company’s portfolio. In addition, I will point out some downside risks that investors should consider when managing their EXEL position. Finally, I take a look at the Daily Chart to see if I can identify any opportunities for investors.

Background on Exelixis

Exelixis is an oncology company that has become a leader in renal cell carcinoma (RCC), while potentially expanding their expertise in neuroendocrine tumor (NET), and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) thanks to their flagship TKI, CABOMETYX. As the only approved product, CABOMETYX has been the primary breadwinner but has quickly achieved blockbuster status with impressive profitability metrics, which have allowed the company to be aggressive with injecting their cash flow into their pipeline. However, Exelixis might have another ace in their deck with their next-gen TKI, zanzalintinib, which could make them an instant contender in colorectal cancer (CRC).

What is more, like CABOMETYX, zanzalintinib has the potential to collect multiple indications and lines of therapy. As a result, zanzalintinib could become the company’s "second" backbone therapy.

I must also point out that the company has earlier-stage small molecules, immunotherapies, and antibody drug conjugates that not only target other cancer types but also have the potential to be operative against some of CABOMETYX’s and zanzalintinib’s target cancer types. So, these younger pipeline assets have the potential to expand the company’s business but also strengthen their current expertise.

In terms of overall health, Exelixis appears to have built a self-sustaining machine that has strong cash flow and has amassed a $1.06B cash position while also supporting nearly $1B in annual R&D, as well as share repurchases. So, I see a company that has the ingredients and structure to graduate to a multi-franchise oncology company.

Recent Performance

The company’s Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings report revealed that CABOMETYX continues to dominate with $2.123B in U.S. net product revenue for 2025, which was a 17% increase over 2024. Q4’s revenue came in at $598.66M, which was a slight improvement over Q4 of 2024. As a result, the company reported mixed earnings for Q4, with a beat on EPS but a slight miss on revenue. However, I must point out that the company’s earnings history does show that Exelixis has been on an upward trajectory for 5 years and has roughly doubled their revenue and EPS during that timeframe.

Obviously, this is thanks to CABOMETYX’s expansion beyond RCC, with NET tumors generating over $100M in revenue in 2025. CABOMETYX is now the market leader for TKIs, with roughly 46% prescription share, and is the most prescribed TKI-IO combo in first-line RCC. So, CABOMETYX should be seen as an entrenched drug that will be hard to disrupt.

Looking at the company’s 2026 guidance below, we can see that management is expecting another strong year with revenue hitting $2.52B-$2.62B, but that is not including any potential revenue from zanzalintinib.

Yes, zanzalintinib’s PDUFA date is in December, so it is logical not to include any potential revenue, but it is important to highlight because the company is expecting another strong year of double-digit revenue growth with their current business.

CABOMETYX and Zanzalintinib Dynamic

CABOMETYX has dominated the Exelixis thesis for years and is still the flagship product. However, the potential addition of zanzalintinib could add a level to CABOMETYX's durable cash-generating foundation.

CABOMETYX's strength in RCC and growth in NET could allow it to hit its peak sales estimates of $2.8B by 2029. At that point, zanzalintinib should be approaching blockbuster status, with peak sales being estimated at around $5B by 2033. If we assume the company will hit these estimates, we could see Exelixis pull in over $7B in less than a decade. Using both peak sales estimates of $7B and a conservative price-to-sales of 4x-5x (biotech is about 6x price-to-sales), we would get roughly $108-$135 per share. Considering EXEL is trading around $44 per share, I think it is appropriate to say that the ticker has significant upside if CABOMETYX and zanzalintinib can get some overlap near their peak. Even if they miss by a year or two, we could still be looking at two blockbuster drugs.

Indeed, I know some believe that zanzalintinib will cannibalize some CABOMETYX revenue, but zanzalintinib is not being positioned to be a one-for-one replacement. Zanzalintinib should be more than that, so even if it completely consumes all of CABOMETYX’s market, we are still looking at a blockbuster drug with greater growth potential. Plus, investors need to see that CABOMETYX has already built a profitable company that has stashed away cash while also committing to R&D and even share repurchases.

So, even if zanzalintinib ends up just being a slightly better version of CABOMETYX… we are still looking at a highly profitable company with an expansive pipeline of assets to keep the growth narrative alive.

Risks to Consider

Despite my bullishness on Exelixis, I must admit that investors have a few downside risks that they need to consider when managing their position. First, we have to consider the clinical execution risk around zanzalintinib. The company's future is relying on zanzalintinib not only fueling growth but also absorbing the eventual loss of CABOMETYX's exclusivity. Yes, the data has been encouraging across multiple Phase III trials in different tumor types and settings, but a couple of misses and that $5B peak sales estimate could be cut substantially, and the second franchise thesis might be trashed.

Second, we have to consider the commercial execution risk, which will increase as the company looks to expand into GI oncology. The company has been strong in GU cancers like RCC, but we don't know if they can accelerate in GI with their current sales infrastructure. Exelixis may struggle to penetrate new physician networks, and zanzalintinib's launch could be lackluster.

We also have to keep an eye on the competition, which includes big pharma players, including Merck (MRK). These heavy hitters have the expertise, finances, relationships, and influence to bully some of the smaller players around on the market. Consequently, investors need to accept that Exelixis may have a superior product, but they might struggle to maximize its commercial potential.

I must also stress that Exelixis will have to continue to invest heavily in combination therapies and novel mechanisms to maintain their growth trajectory and appease the market. CABOMETYX’s loss of exclusivity is projected to be around 2030. So, the company is going to need zanzalintinib to grow faster than CABOMETYX shrinks, or they need one of their pipeline programs to be a success and contribute.

Despite these risks, I’m maintaining my EXEL conviction rating at 4 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare “Bioreactor” growth portfolio.

Finding an Opportunity

The big catalyst remaining on the 2026 calendar is zanzalintinib’s PDUFA date, which is set for December 3rd for third-line CRC, which could be the drug’s first FDA approval.

If approved, Exelixis will then have a commercial launch for CRC but will simultaneously have several supplementary data readouts from their STELLAR-303 subgroups and STELLAR-304 (nccRCC) that will reveal the drug’s ability to broaden the label.

In addition, the company will be pushing another wave of STELLAR pivotal trials (311, 316, 201) and getting data on some of zanzalintinib’s combination therapies, which will be essential to getting into earlier lines of therapy. Therefore, post-PDUFA could see an acceleration both commercially and clinically that might reveal if zanzalintinib has the prospects to scale to a franchise drug ahead of CABOMETYX’s loss of exclusivity.

Considering these points, I’m considering adding to my dormant EXEL position that hasn’t been touched since October of 2022, when the ticker was trading around $16 per share. Now, EXEL is trading above $44 per share and is showing a strong setup to test the ticker’s all-time high of $49.40.

I can see that EXEL is bullish on the Go-No-Go indicator and is trading above all anchored VWAPs, as well as the 200-Day EMA. However, EXEL failed to hold the breakout above the downtrend ray from the all-time high, so I’m either going to wait and see if it can break out and close above that downtrend ray or if it wants to drop and bounce off the anchored VWAP from February before considering a buy.

Admittedly, I typically have a more systematic approach to handling my positions, but EXEL’s momentum and the company’s growth trajectory have made it difficult to calculate my traditional Buy and Sell Levels. So, I’m going to keep a close eye on EXEL over the next couple of months to see if the Daily Chart gives me enough information to formulate my strategy. Until then, I’m only committing to comically small additions on technical momentum.

Long-term, my objective is to trade this position in order to create profit and build a "house money" core position that has been financed by the market to hold as a long-term investment.

data.title: ASML Q1 Earnings Review: Sales Growth Slows, Memory Dominates Compute
data.description:
ASML Overview

Dutch semiconductor manufacturing equipment provider ASML Holding N.V.’s (ASML) stock price didn't generate a substantial investor sentiment despite its Q1 earnings release on the 15th of April indicating that it had exceeded expectations with €8.8 billion in net sales and €2.76 billion in net profit.

The factors behind this are centered on the overall expectations of the AI Hype cycle, to which ASML has been linked to.

Trend Drilldown

Early trends indicate that net sales (or revenue) could close Fiscal Year (FY) 2026 at roughly half the growth exhibited in FY 2025.

The bulk of the slowdown can be attributed to the sale of lithography machines used in the manufacturing of advanced chips. However, as past data reveals, this is a highly cyclical business: the machines sold are expensive, long-lived and go on to generate income for the company via service fees, et al, which are captured in the "Service and Field Option Sales" line item and which is trending at 20% growth in FY2026.

Expenses such as R&D expenditure and selling costs are running at par with the previous FY while the relatively higher ticket price of machines sold are aiding in boosting net income to trend at 16% growth which is, again, lower than that seen through most of the 2020s.

A very noticeable change, at least in this quarter, is the composition of the purpose for which machines are being acquired by clients: memory manufacturing dominates over the sale of machines for the manufacturing of GPUs ("Graphical Computation Units") that are the core of the "brain" employed for running AI.

Within a single quarter, machines for the manufacturing of memory chips have gone from 30% of all system sales to 51%. This is borne out by the massive increase in sales share in South Korea from 22% to 45%, where Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and SK Hynix (HXSCL) have been ramping up production of memory chips. Furthermore, of relevance is the fact pattern that EUV ("extreme ultraviolet") lithography machines comprise 66% of all sales now (up from 48%). ASML is the world's sole supplier of EUV machines employed in the manufacturing of advanced chips. While companies such as Japan’s Nikon and Canon supply lithography tools as well, they are generally deemed not sufficiently capable enough for the thin-wafer technology being employed in AI-relevant chips.

Now, while EUV machines find particular favor for the manufacturing of AI-relevant chips, the dynamics are different for memory chips. Memory manufacturers typically use EUV for critical layers and rely heavily on DUV ("Deep Ultraviolet") lithography machines for other layers. During the earnings call, CFO Roger Dassen highlighted that DUV would be an area of interest for the company. While the company leads in high-end DUV ("immersion") and EUV, it faces intense competition in the DUV space: Nikon, for instance, is a particularly staunch competitor while Canon has been actively advocating for cost-effective alternative technologies to the type of high-end DUV that both ASML and Nikon exist in. In the "non-EUV" business, Dassen continues, the company expect to see an increase in demand in 2026 as opposed to previous expectations of running flat relative to previous FY.

As a percentage of sales, however, it bears noting the company shows quite strong symmetry across FYs:

While net income has improved as a result of higher-margin EUV sales, they do not project massive impacts in passthrough efficiency. The company essentially prices to perfection and collects on installed capacity like one would collect on an annuity.

Consequences of Control and Concentration

ASML also indicated that China has undergone a near-halving of revenue share in Q1 2206 relative to Q4 2025 as the company seemingly limits sales to previously-inked contracts (as indicated in the previous earnings call) under weight of technology export restrictions. This represents a long-term bottleneck for the company: its ability to turn out high-quality products effectively turns it into a pawn of global geopolitics, potentially leaving a significant portion of demand untapped.

This untapped demand, in turn, becomes the potential nucleus of permanent substitution. Given that compute and memory are the cornerstones of technology employed in modern economies, national demands must be met. In countries with high friction, sovereign policymaking trends and large talent pools, this will inevitably promote calls for self-reliance. Restrictions on China or even a "soft ban" on (for example) Indian enterprises acquiring the technology to meet domestic needs in the future under the banner of geopolitical wrangling will inevitably lead to the creation of alternatives.

Proponents of ASML who mention "moats" might like to bear in mind that moats surrounding castles were regularly breached in the Middle Ages. While companies think in quarters and fiscal years, countries necessarily think in decades at least.

Another aspect that investors are likely considering is the sustainability of demand. Purchases of ASML's machines are predicated on sustained demand for AI-relevant chips in datacenters, which is currently under fire in arguably one of the world's biggest market for AI: the U.S. The state of Maine has passed a "temporary" ban on the construction of datacenters. This was merely the legislative affirmation of a larger trend: between May 2024 and early 2026, local opposition in the U.S. has significantly impacted the data center industry, with $64 billion to $162 billion in projects being blocked or delayed.

The grassroots of the movement against datacenters have a strong bipartisan flavor: around 55% of the local officials opposed to datacenters are Republicans (i.e.. ostensibly the same party in power in the White House). The factors for this opposition have been well-documented in recent times: datacenters consume massive volumes of water and electricity that often has the communities they're situated in - usually rural and relatively impoverished - reeling from the effects of and footing the bill for.

In contrast: the likes of China and India might have strategic intents behind building datacenters (as opposed to the mostly commercial in the U.S.). However, given their economic size and talent availability, owning a stake in production of assets is a point of contention they would seek to negotiate but ASML is unable to comply with, given the laws it must follow. Whether ASML would ever be able to is currently deemed as being somewhere between uncertain and unlikely.

The uncertainty behind widespread adoption given the high costs, the grassroots opposition, and the geopolitical constraints are combining to create an impact on the forward valuation on the likes of ASML and potentially in other AI-adjacent stocks in this current earnings season.

Being forewarned is being forearmed. All in all, a Hold is in order.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: GOOY: Income Finally Matters As Google Enters A Slower Phase
data.description:
It's been a year since I wrote about the YieldMax GOOGL Option Income Strategy ETF (GOOY). At the time of the earlier thesis, markets were crashing due to tariff uncertainties. I had taken a Hold stance primarily because single stock-based covered call writing strategies cannot be seen in isolation—the underlying moves do impact the total returns trajectory of the income variant. And GOOY did not look like a route for total returns, especially with Google already at the lows at that point.

That picture has changed today. Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) has already rallied by ~100% over the past year. And it has been consolidating since October. Theoretically, that could be a time when covered calls become more relevant and can generate income on their own, even if the underlying goes nowhere. GOOY's structure shows a call spread setup, which ensures sharp upside can be captured reasonably well. That means premium harvesting is compromised to an extent, but GOOY somewhat offsets that trade-off by an aggressive call coverage seen today. All in all, given where we are in the Google capex cycle and expectations of a flatter regime for the next few quarters, GOOY could be a decent tool to generate stable income.

How GOOY Is Set Up

I downloaded GOOY's full holdings as of April 15th, 2026, and saw around 6.6k contracts set up as a synthetic long exposure (selling puts and buying strikes at a $310 strike). That accounts for the fund's AUM too at ~$210 million (6.6 thousand contracts x 100 multiple x ~$320-$340 share price range now). The covered call layer is implemented as a spread. Calls at near strikes are written (across 3 strikes ranging from $317.5 to $322.5—with ~70% of the writing activity at the farthest strike). For each call written, a call is bought $7.5 away.

That's a laddered call spread structure, which means net premiums received are lower than simply selling covered calls. The ladder and spread setup ensures that any sharp upside is well captured, though. Obviously, the most aggressive income setup is 100% covered call writing at the money and no spreads, and GOOY sacrifices some income potential to enable upside capture. That stance has paid off too over the past year, when GOOY was able to capture ~80% upside when Google doubled, very strong upside capture for a covered call strategy. But it could mean a less optimal setup for a range-bound market, where higher net premiums augur well for income.

GOOY balances the act, though. While net premiums are sacrificed to an extent, it writes options for the full portfolio (adding up the positions, as seen in the table below). Several option writing strategies write options for only part of the underlying portfolio. The other strategy that matters is how close the chosen strikes are to the spot price. While GOOY's current strikes are deep ITM, the share price did move to ~$275 over the past month as markets turned defensive during the peak of the Iran tensions before rebounding to ~$330 now, making any assessment of the current strikes difficult. How aggressively they were set up, in terms of proximity to the spot prices, depends on when they were set up—strikes being deep ITM today is not sufficient to conclude that GOOY is positioning its strikes aggressively.

All in all, GOOY's portfolio coverage for option writing is definitely aggressive, and somewhat offsets the spread setup that aims to keep sharp upside scenarios open. The net effect of that stance is that we have a strategy that does balance income in flat conditions with an ultimate reliance on Google's upside over time.

The Google Thesis

That setup means the Google Thesis becomes important. GOOY funds its income from both capital appreciation of the underlying as well as option income. The latter becomes prominent only when Google is going through a consolidation phase, which is now. In my last Google analysis, I argued for the same consolidation phase ahead because returns from here appear to be less about rerating and more about earnings catching up. The margin of safety also looks lower at ~27x earnings, especially because a heavy CapEx cycle raises the burden of proof. Here is the crux of my stand on Google, not negative, but a phase where large upside moves should be less expected:

The upcoming quarters will be more about monetization scrutiny after a long build phase. Google is transitioning into a phase where at least some Cloud earnings are funding capex, and initial signals are returning positives more than not. So yes, the capex cycle is not a bearish development. It is just a mature transition phase, where I do not see the case for an easy 20-30% gain over the next year. In fact, risks are elevated, because when capital intensity nearly doubles, valuation sensitivity increases.

And to be fair, that regime is already playing out now over the past 4-5 months (through volatile moves on both sides). So, this is not really a prediction—it is already a consolidation in action.

Expectations Ahead

Given the flatter Google outlook ahead, the option income in GOOY should work better than what we have seen in the past year. And in case Google rallies, GOOY is well equipped to capture a lot of that upside too. Hence I rate GOOY as a Buy now. However, investors should set expectations right. As I have highlighted before, the cost of keeping upside open means the income generation in flat to pressured markets is often in basis points a month in flat conditions. The chart below shows how GOOY has at best added 3-4 percentage points at the March 2026 bottom.

Overall, expect gains to be in the range of high single digits over a year or so if Google stays flat or corrects further. That is not sufficient to compensate for sharp downsides, which is why a long-term bullish stance in Google will still be required. The thesis flips to a Buy because the option layer starts working now, Google's downside is looking limited, and preparing for a slower regime ahead is prudent. In bullish regimes, GOOY can still deliver but falls short of Google's own upside. But in current conditions, GOOY can make its own mark—the very strategy that enhances income should come into play.

data.title: Radiant Logistics: I Was Wrong, But That Doesn't Make It A 'Buy' (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In January, I downgraded 3PL provider Radiant Logistics (RLGT) from "Buy" to "Sell." Since then, the stock has surged 21.9%, and so you can view this as a horrible "Sell" call from my side. When stocks move so strongly against my rating, it is not a reason for me to ignore things but to carefully look at what I may have missed. In this report, I will do that and update my price target for Radiant Logistics.

Radiant Logistics Earnings Decline But Margins Remain Stable

In the second quarter of FY26, revenues decreased 12.3% to $232.1 million, missing analyst estimates by $3.3 million. Operating income declined 14.2% to $7.5 million. Those are seemingly not impressive results, but they are also not horrible. We note that operating expenses declined more or less in line with revenues, with the cost of transportation, SG&A, and depreciation declining by 16.2%, 19.9%, and 29.2%, respectively, offset by higher personnel costs and operating partner commissions. As a result, operating margins were relatively stable at 3.22% compared to 3.3% a year ago. That is definitely a strong sign. In a depressed market, 3PL providers typically see margins of 1%-3%, and that is where Radiant Logistics is sitting right now.

We also note that Radiant Logistics had non-recurring emergency air charter revenues in the comparable period last year. If we adjust for that, we get a better view of the recurring performance. Adjusted EBITDA grew from $6.1 million to $11.8 million, and that actually shows that the company did extremely well on its recurring business.

The Mixed Picture From Middle East Disruptions

With capacity tightening due to the Middle East reducing vessels and airplanes in operation, the capacity for shipping has tightened further, amplified by rerouting of ships and airplanes. Combined with higher oil prices, this leads to higher freight rates and a more complex logistics chain to navigate. That provides opportunities for brokers and adoption of Radiant’s Navegate platform as customers are looking for better visibility and cost optimization. However, at the same time, the secondary effect that I believe the market is insufficiently considering is that economic growth may stall, and that would reduce demand for shipping and brokerage services. Currently, it seems like a lock grid does have economic impact but also keeps capacity tight and thereby keeps freight rates elevated. So, the dynamics are seemingly moderately bullish, although I tend to lean towards believing that the macro picture could be grim.

Radiant Logistics Stock Upgraded To "Hold"

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Radiant Logistics, I implemented the better recurring business in the valuation, and that leads to a $7.40 price target. That indicates downside, but I am moving the rating from "Sell" to "Hold." The reason is that in my prior assessment I implemented a manual override to account for the freight recession risk that I saw, even though the stock did show high single-digit upside. I am now moving the rating from "Sell" to "Hold" in anticipation of any transient opportunities that tightening capacity may have for Radiant Logistics.

For Radiant Logistics, the margins for this year still do not quite look impressive, but with the strong performance so far and the strength observed in the recurring business, I have increased margin assumptions by 60 basis points this year and 20 basis points next year. The hidden lever for Radiant Logistics is the fact that it has no major near-term debt. Combined with positive free cash flow, that should drive up the cash position, which the company can use to acquire companies. This is the typical freight playbook where times of weakness are used to inorganically grow the business.

Conclusion: Radiant Logistics Navigates A Tough Environment

The earnings that Radiant Logistics provided showed good cost control, and we note that the company’s recurring business looked substantially stronger than expected in the first half of the year. What the second half of the year will bring is not quite known. On one hand, tightening capacity due to the war in the Middle East could drive margins for brokers and increase demand for Navegate, which provides customers with routing and capacity purchasing options. The risk, however, is that reduced consumer sentiment and macro growth tapering could result in lower volumes. For now, I am upgrading the stock from "Sell" to "Hold."

data.title: FTXL Presents Growth Across All Aspects Of The Semiconductor Industry
data.description:
The First Trust Nasdaq Semiconductor ETF (FTXL) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund designed to track the performance of the US semiconductor market. With major announcements for large data center developments, semiconductor sourcing, and advanced fabrication, FTXL may be an appealing investment strategy for those seeking diversified exposure to semiconductor designers, fabricators, and capital equipment manufacturers. Given the robust market outlook, I am recommending FTXL with a Buy rating.

Investment Thesis for FTXL

The semiconductor market entered 2026 with a lot of uncertainty regarding the viability of the AI growth theme, effectively exhibiting flat performance early on in the year before gaining momentum. With the emerging war in Iran, concerns regarding helium supply quickly gained attention by the market, though I do not believe that this supply constraint will be as impactful as initially expected given the US’s excess supply of natural gas from which helium is fractionated.

I believe momentum in the industry is picking up with one central theme that sprouts into several market segments:AI data center growth

The headline factor is tied to companies like Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Marvell Technology (MRVL), Micron Technology (MU), and Intel Corp. (INTC) from an AI chip designer perspective. In addition to these names, Qualcomm (QCOM) announced a new series of chips to compete in the AI accelerator market in late-2025. Driving growth in the advanced computing corner of the semiconductor market is the driving need for compute capacity, particularly when considering the gradually increasing adoption of agentic AI and the transition towards inferencing. The Wall Street Journal recently reported that cloud services providers have been increasing GPU rental prices given the significant demand for compute capacity. In short: demand continues to outstrip supply.

This means that the investment cycle is likely far from over, particularly when considering the transition from designing infrastructure to support general workloads vs. purpose-built infrastructure for specific workloads. This last point is more tied to Broadcom’s custom silicon solutions being designed to spec for their respective hyperscaler and AI developer customers. While I expect Broadcom to take on more market share in the coming years given its agreements to deploy multiple gigawatts of capacity across its hyperscaler customers, I do not believe that this will be destructive to Nvidia’s growth trajectory; it should be more considered as an accompaniment.

Another corner of the semiconductor market FTXL covers includes the analog and power chip designers and fabricators, including ON Semiconductor Corporation (ON), Texas Instruments (TXN), and Analog Devices (ADI), amongst others. This market is entering a new growth cycle as power-related infrastructure is being placed at a rapid scale to support power needs by large-scale data centers. At a high level, these chips support power management and distribution, ensuring stable flows of electricity to be distributed across the data center environment, playing a critical role for the “gray space” in the data center, or the non-IT infrastructure. Utilities are expected to invest $1.4T in grid modernization and expansion projects over the next 5 years, an appealing outlook for these chipmakers.

Enabling chip manufacturing is the semi-cap equipment manufacturers, including KLA Corp. (KLAC), Lam Research Corp. (LRCX), Applied Materials (AMAT), and Teradyne (TER). While this subsector plays a smaller role in the portfolio, the theme remains appealing when considering the overall investment initiative of FTXL. These companies develop the machines that fabricate and test the wafers, playing a critical role in the chip fabrication lifecycle. A number of chip fabrication projects are underway with Micron expanding its fabrication facilities to support higher demand for HBM memory chips. Intel recently announced an agreement with Tesla (TSLA) to develop its Terafab, which is envisioned to fabricate 1mm chips per month when fully ramped up. With greater urgency for advanced chip production, I believe the semi-cap manufacturers will realize substantial growth over the coming years, outfitting fabrication facilities with advanced chipmaking infrastructure.

Overall, I believe the semiconductor market is poised for continued growth over the coming years supported by market demand for advanced semiconductors and power infrastructure. FTXL covers the broad semiconductor market, including companies that cater to both of these themes, and has the ability to deliver strong growth as a component of an investor’s diversified portfolio.

About First Trust Nasdaq Semiconductor ETF

FTXL was launched by First Trust Advisors on September 20, 2016 on the Nasdaq Exchange. The strategy has a total expense ratio of 60bps, a moderate premium when compared to thematic ETFs managed by Invesco, State Street, and iShares.

Comparing total returns, FTXL has performed exceptionally well in recent years with the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) as the top performer of this cohort.

By comparison, FTXL exhibits strong liquidity with $1.87b in assets under management with $30mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis; SMH exhibits greater depth with $52b in net assets and $3.6b in share value trading daily. SMH also exhibits a lower expense ratio of 35bps, making it a strong alternative for FTXL.

FTXL was designed to track the performance of the Nasdaq US Smart Semiconductor™ Index, a dynamic Index that assesses volatility, value, and growth in addition to utilizing a modified factor-weighted methodology for security selection and weighting. In accordance with its weighting methodology, the Index evaluates profitability, return on assets, trading momentum, and relative value based on cash flow. Securities in the Index are capped at an 8% weight.

Risks Related to FTXL

FTXL is a sector-focused equity strategy designed to track the performance of the broader semiconductor market, exposing investors to potential risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. The analog chip market has historically exhibited cyclicality and may continue this historical trend going forward. Growth across the semiconductor industry is underpinned by AI data center growth; if growth were to slow down or stall, the semiconductor market could face substantial headwinds, particularly when considering the amount of chip fabrication being put in place to support the expected demand.

Final Thoughts

FTXL is an appealing investment strategy for those seeking diversified exposure to the semiconductor market. With multiple themes presenting as positive catalysts to the market, I believe FTXL can exhibit strong growth for the coming years. I am recommending FTXL with a Buy rating as a component of a diversified portfolio strategy; investors may consider SMH as a viable alternative.

data.title: Hemab Therapeutics Begins IPO Effort On Positive Trial Results
data.description:
A Quick Take On Hemab Therapeutics’ IPO

Hemab Therapeutics Holdings, Inc. (COAG) has filed to raise pipeline development capital from an IPO of its common stock, per an S-1 registration statement.

The company is a clinical-stage biopharma developing treatments for various blood coagulation disorders.

COAG has produced positive trial efficacy results so far, its management is experienced, it has relationships with major pharma firms and is well capitalized.

The IPO should be in demand from institutional life science investors.

Hemab Therapeutics Overview And Development Efforts

Cambridge, Massachusetts-based Hemab is developing a pipeline of treatments for blood-clotting conditions and has programs in various stages of trials, discovery and pre-clinical studies.

The firm is developing antibody-based treatments that modulate the body’s coagulation system, rather than replacing missing clotting factors.

Hemab’s current pipeline status is shown in the graphic below:

The company's lead program is expecting to begin Phase 3 trials in the second half of 2026.

Its Phase 2 trial results for the treatment of Glansmann thrombasthenia included that a “reduction in mean annualized treated bleed rate, or ATBR, for patients treated with sutacimig ranged from 19% to 87%, indicating clinical activity at all regimens tested.”

Management of Hemab is led by President and Chief Executive Officer, Mr. Benny Sørensen, M.D., Ph.D, who has been with the company since 2021 and was previously SVP Strategic Projects at Codiak Biosciences. He has held numerous other senior roles in life science companies throughout his career.

Institutional investors in Hemab include RA Capital Management, Novo Holdings, AI DEN-MAB, Sofinnova, Deep Track Capital, SMALLCAP World Fund, Healthcap and Avoro Ventures, and have invested a fair market value of approximately $365 million to date.

Hemab’s Market & Competitors

The firm is operating in the bleeding disorders market, which Allied Market Research said was valued at $13.8 billion in 2022, according to a market research report.

Allied forecasted the global market for treatments to grow to $26 billion by 2032.

If accomplished, this growth would represent a CAGR of 6.6% from 2023 to 2032, a moderate rate of growth.

Growth will likely come from an aging population suffering from a higher incidence of bleeding disorders.

The major disease types treated by drugs are shown in the graphic here:

Competitors in the market, which include legacy incumbents and newer market participants, include:

  • Medexus Pharma (MEDXF)

  • Takeda Pharmaceutical (TAK)

  • Octapharma AG

  • CSL Limited (CSLLY)

  • Sanofi (SNY)

  • Grifols (GRFS)

  • Bayer AG (BAYRY)

  • Novo Nordisk (NVO)

  • Pfizer (PFE)

  • Roche (RHHBY)

The bleeding disorders market is seeing a shift in development toward preventative therapies rather than just episodic treatment.

Barriers to advancement include limited patient populations that make trial recruiting difficult and strong IP and biologics manufacturing challenges.

Hemab’s approach is to focus on subcutaneous preventive therapies, which are more patient-friendly, and to target unmet needs with no existing approved preventive options.

Hemab’s Financial Results

The firm’s financial results are typical of a clinical-stage biopharma, since they show no revenue and substantial R&D and G&A costs associated with its pipeline development efforts, per the graphic below:

As of the end of 2025, Hemab had $185.5 million in cash, equivalents and marketable securities and $11.25 million in total liabilities and no debt.

At its current operating cash usage, the company would have about 24 months of runway.

However, with it entering costly Phase 3 trials, that runway is likely far shorter, so the IPO’s proceeds, combined with its existing resources, will probably provide it with a total of 18 - 24 months of runway at a much higher burn rate in the near future.

IPO Details

Hemab has filed a placeholder figure of $100 million for the SEC review fee related to the offering of its common stock.

Shareholder sale lock-ups will be for the typical 180-day period following the effective date of the prospectus, which the SEC determines after its review process is completed.

Hemab said it intends to utilize the IPO net proceeds as described below:

to fund clinical development of sutacimig for the treatment of Glanzmann thrombasthenia and Factor VII deficiency;

to fund clinical development of HMB-002 for the treatment of Von Willebrand Disease;

for additional discovery, preclinical, and clinical activities for current and future programs; and

the remainder for working capital and other general corporate purposes.

(Source: SEC)

Management said the company is not subject to any material legal proceedings.

The listed underwriters of the IPO include Goldman Sachs, Jefferies, Evercore ISI, and Wedbush PacGrow.

My Thoughts About Hemab’s IPO

Hemab is a phase 3 trial stage biopharma seeking US public capital market investment in advancing its pipeline and new treatment discovery and pre-clinical studies efforts.

The firm is led by an experienced senior executive team and has a strong life science institutional investor syndicate.

Hemab is well-capitalized but is entering the high cash-burn stage of its pipeline development, so it will need large sums of money to continue its development efforts.

The challenge for management is to produce valuation increases in advance of needing additional funding in order to show progress and command more funding without undue dilution of shareholders.

The market it is seeking to penetrate is large and expected to grow at a moderate rate of growth.

It features incumbents and upstarts, of which Hemab is definitely an aspiring entrant.

COAG faces the challenges of finding suitable patient populations for its trial efforts.

Also, it will owe milestone, royalty and other payments to Novo Nordisk and/or Genmab, under its license agreements with those entities.

The extent of those future payments will negative impact its margins, but that will be some years in the future.

Hemab is also an ‘emerging growth company’ and ‘smaller reporting company’, which enables management to produce less information for shareholders.

A number of these types of company stocks have performed poorly post-IPO, although they have typically been more prevalent in the general technology industries.

The US biopharma industry IPOs have been increasingly bifurcated in their post-IPO returns in recent years.

So, life science IPO investors have typically either done very well or very badly in the quarters after the IPO.

With reasonable valuation assumptions, the IPO should be in demand from institutional life science investors.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be determined

data.title: Citizens Financial: Solid Q1 Shows No Signs Of Economic Weakness
data.description:
We are early in our coverage of the Q1 regional bank earnings season. We believe reviewing the earnings of regional banks is a great way to do a "pulse check" on the strength of the economy both locally and regionally. Thus far we have seen strength in the few that have reported thus far.

In today's column, we return to Citizens Financial Group, Inc. (CFG). We maintain a neutral rating at these levels, and note that operations are remaining resilient. Citizens is one of the larger regional banks that we cover. A few years ago management embarked on a strategy to be more strict in its lending standards and this paid off with improving asset quality over the last few years. While there was some pressure on loan volume from the tightening of standards, the margin power has gotten better. Further, the bank stock continues to raise its dividend, with another 9.5% increase announced last fall. Today Citizens just-reported Q1 earnings. Let us check in on and discuss the key metrics of Citizens Financial that we follow.

Citizens Financial Q1 Earnings Headline Results

In the just-reported Q1, Citizens saw positive results overall. Revenues rose a robust 11.9% to $2.17 billion from a year ago. It was essentially in line with consensus expectations (a beat of $10 million). The provision for credit losses was $140 million, which was down from $153 million from last year's Q1. We see no issue with this level of provisions which could indicate issues with repayment or expected losses. In fact, loan loss provisions for losses have been around the same level each quarter over the last year and are edging lower over time. That is a good sign.

The bank saw net income increase from $373 million last year to $517 million in Q1. It did however edge slightly lower from $528 million in the sequential Q4. But EPS was better than expected, It hit $1.13 and was a $0.04 beat against consensus expectations. This was up 47% from last year last year and was in line with the sequential Q4. As noted above, with tightening standards, deposits and loans had declined for many quarters. But they are now stabilizing and moving in the right direction.

Citizens Financial Q1 Loans and Deposits

This quarter, average deposits were up to $181.3 billion versus $179.9billion in the sequential quarter. They were also up from $172.7 billion a year ago. So, it seems the long decline in deposits has stopped and now reversed over the last year. This is very positive news, as lending standards remain strict. That strict lending criteria drove total loans down for many quarters, but loans have started to increase again over the last year. That said, loans at period-end were $143.7 million in Q1, up from $142.7 million starting the quarter. Here is the recent trend:

This is positive. Growth in loans and deposits is key for any regional bank that we cover, we now have a return to growth.

Citizens Financial Q1 Asset Quality and Return Metrics

Asset quality has stabilized as well, and 2025 as a whole enjoyed a nice trend for asset quality. We want to point out that net interest margin of 3.14% was up 8 basis points from the sequential Q4, which was positive. Margins back above 3% are a huge improvement. Net interest margin was up 25 basis points from a year ago. This is helping drive earnings strength.

Net loan charge-offs are also trending lower, and that is a sign of strength. These were $138 million, $17 million lower than in the sequential Q4, and down from $175 million a year ago. Thus, net charge-offs were just 0.39% as a percentage of all loans, the lowest it has been in a year.

This is a solid trend folks. Nonaccrual loans were $1.5 billion and were down modestly from the sequential Q4. There was improvement in commercial, largely C&I, with minor increases in retail loans, largely mortgages.

So better loan balances, higher deposits, and improving credit metrics. This is a sign of strength. One minor blemish was that the efficiency ratio softened to 63.6% from 62.2% in the sequential Q4. But this is an improvement from last year's 67.9%.

Finally, the return metrics are in strong shape and held mostly firm from the sequential quarter. The underlying return on average tangible assets was 0.97% vs. 0.98% in the sequential quarter and was up nicely from 0.73% last year. The underlying return on average equity hit 12.19% versus 12.18% in the sequential Q4, holding firm at this elevated level. Notably it was up from 9.64% a year ago.

Final thoughts

Citizens Financial Group, Inc's strategy to tighten up lending standards a few years ago led to a number of quarters of adjustment. But it is back in growth mode. We are seeing increases in loan balances, higher deposits, and improving credit metrics. Return metrics are as good as they have been in years. We see no signs of economic weakness in this report. Overall, we view CFG stock as a quality Hold.

data.title: NXP Semiconductors Q1 Preview: Auto Disappoints, But A New Growth Driver Is Coming
data.description:
NXP Semiconductors N.V. (NXPI) is due to report its Q1 ’26 earnings on the 28th of April, so I thought I’d look into what to expect and what I am looking for to give us a better look at the company’s potential and whether it would be a good time to start a position.

Since the last article on the company, its share price declined by around 9% against 2% gain for the SPY (SPY). I don’t see anything particularly exciting yet, so I maintain my Hold rating until we hear more about the next big growth driver.

What to Expect

Analysts are expecting the company to make around $2.98 and $3.52 in adjusted and GAAP EPS, respectively, which is the opposite of what we usually see with these numbers. Additionally, they expect the company to make $3.15B in revenues, which translates to around 11% growth y/y, but around 6% decline sequentially. It is worth noting that there have been 14 upward and 6 downward revisions to the EPS over the last 90 days. These numbers are in line with what the management guided for the upcoming quarter.

Over the last ten quarters, the company only missed twice when it came to revenues and essentially met or beat EPS estimates 100% of the time. A miss by a penny is negligible.

I think there is a good chance that we will see the company beating these estimates once again because it looks like the management either consistently low-balls its guidance or the analysts are underestimating NXPI’s earnings potential. Either way, it’s a positive for the company. Judging by the historical evidence, the beat is not going to be mind-blowing, so I don’t expect a massive share price swing on the day.

So, what that means for my fellow options traders is that if you want to play any direction on the day, the implied move is around d 3.6%, which is quite weak. So, being a seller of options will most likely pay off if you sell far out of money strikes on an iron condor or a strangle, as IV crush will take care of the rest, but just be careful with strangles because you could get wrecked if the move is around 10% on the day. IV crush and theta decay will not be able to offset the increase in short gamma in that case. If you are looking to buy an out-of-money call or put option, it’s essentially a lottery ticket, so it is up to you. Always look for options that have enough volume, so the spread is tight.

What I’m looking for

The biggest item I will be looking out for in the upcoming earnings call is the move NXPI is making into Physical AI and robotics. This is a massive pivot into one of the more promising technology segments these days. This will no doubt give a decent boost to the company’s Industrial and IoT revenue segment, but since it is still in its early innings, I am not expecting miracles just yet. I want to hear some commentary regarding its partnership with Nvidia (NVDA) for physical AI and what kind of TAM they are looking to push into.

NXP said that its new physical AI work is built on secure edge platforms, real-time networking, motor control, and machine vision, so the relevant TAM is much closer to semiconductor content in robotics than the full robot market value. If we look at the third-party data, outer TAM for semis in robotics stood at around $11.2B in 2025, which is expected to rise to a whopping $41.2B by 2030. That is a CAGR of 29.7%. Sure, this is very appealing as a growth factor; however, we need to be realistic here and not expect NXPI to capture a lot of that expansion. To be conservative, I am expecting very little in gaining market share in the next few quarters, mainly because the company is not going for the AI accelerators in the robots, but rather it is targeting the edge control stack, connectivity, motor control, and safety. So, the long-term TAM could be somewhere between $2B to $5B, and the number is closer to millions in the short to medium term outlook for NXPI.

Automotive remains the largest revenue contributor by far, and with the weakness that we have seen in this segment, I am really looking forward to the commentary when the management finally says that the segment is starting to see some strength after a long inventory correction. Unfortunately, that has not been the case just yet. The recovery is taking so much longer than expected, but at least the company managed to keep up the revenues at these levels.

With the upbeat guidance from the previous earnings report, many analysts agree that the automotive segment is stabilizing, but are still being quite conservative and do not dare call for an aggressive rebound.

Once again, we just have to hope that there will be a clear improvement in the segment by late 2026, if auto production steadies and restocking finally starts. I’ve been looking forward to this for a couple of years now, and this scenario just never came to fruition, so I am not going to be holding my breath this year. I really don’t expect a marked improvement yet. I rather hope that NXPI’s venture into physical AI is a lot more successful, which will help offset the lackluster top-line performance overall.

Am I looking to buy NXPI before earnings?

On paper, the company seems cheap, with a forward PE ratio of around 15; however, when you think about it, the company’s revenue segments aren’t growing particularly fast, as there seems to be some weakness across the board, especially its largest revenue segment, exhibiting consistently poor performance.

I think I will be holding off for now and waiting to see how the company’s venture into physical AI and robotics pans out. I will be very interested in hearing the potential in this segment from the management in the upcoming conference call. It would be amazing to see that there might be a promising growth segment added to the company’s operations, as long as NXPI can execute the plan to perfection.

data.title: First Horizon: A Decent Start, But Follow Through Is Key
data.description:
It’s still early in the quarterly earnings reporting cycle, and while First Horizon’s (FHN) business appears to be in solid shape, it is unlikely to be among the performance leaders for the quarter. Credit quality remains good and First Horizon still enjoys good share in the attractive Southeastern market, but soft loan growth is a watch item going forward given that spread margin growth is going to be challenging from here.

First Horizon shares are up about 12% since my last update on the bank, which is basically in line with the broader regional bank space and a little better than the average Southeastern bank of comparable size.

At this point I do think First Horizon shares offer some value in a space that doesn’t have a lot of obvious bargains. That said, higher energy prices are a threat to the economy both from the perspective of business activity/investment and rates, so I do see a risk of weaker loan growth across the sector. For investors who can be patient, today’s price still offers a respectable quality/value balance.

A Small Beat, But One That Brings Some Sustainability Questions

First Horizon did beat expectations for the first quarter, with core earnings that were three cents better than expected ($0.53 vs. $0.50) and about a penny better at the core pre-provision earnings line.

Revenue rose over 6% year over year and contracted about 2% quarter over quarter, coming in just a bit below sell-side expectations. Net interest income improved about 6% yoy and contracted 1% qoq, about 1% better than expected (driving a $0.01/share beat), with weaker than expected earning assets (up 3% yoy, almost flat qoq) offset by a stronger than expected net interest margin (up 10bp yoy / 1 bp qoq to 3.52%) that in turn was driven by lower than expected deposit/funding costs.

Fee-based income growth was a little disappointing, growing 9% yoy and contracting 5% qoq and missing by about 5% (or about $0.015/share). Weaker service charges were the main driver relative to sell-side expectations, and it appears that this result was more a byproduct of quarterly “noise” than any fundamental issues.

Core expenses (including amortization) rose less than 4% yoy and contracted more than 7% qoq, coming in about 2% lower than expected and beating by about $0.015/share.

Pre-provision profits rose almost 11% yoy and 6% qoq, beating by about 2%. The ratio of PPOP to average earning assets improved 13bp from the year-ago period, but at about 1.88%, there’s still room for improvement (“good” usually starts at over 2%).

Below the PPOP line, lowering provision expense drove almost $0.03/share of earnings upside, while taxes and share count were both a bit higher than Street expectations.

Weaker Balance Sheet Growth And Limited Rate Leverage Drive Sustainability Questions

The most disappointing part of First Horizon’s first quarter was the weaker-than-expected balance sheet growth. With likely limited ability to drive meaningful upside in NIM in the coming quarters, balance sheet growth is going to be a key driver for earnings growth.

Loans rose a little less than 3% yoy and contracted 0.4% qoq on an average balance basis, and that is likely to stack up as a bit soft compared to other regional banks, and particularly larger banks.

Elevated commercial real estate (or CRE) paydowns are definitely a headwind, but with management pointing to a multiyear high CRE pipeline, this could be abating in the coming quarters. A key here is the health of the multifamily construction market, as this is an important category of CRE lending for First Horizon, but several Southeastern markets have become overbuilt in recent quarters.

Core commercial lending growth of 8% yoy and 0.6% qoq was likewise a little disappointing. I want to see what other Southeastern-focused banks report, but it does appear that commercial lending has been stronger with multinational corporations than with smaller businesses, and that typically favors the largest banks like Bank of America (BAC), JPMorgan (JPM), PNC (PNC), et al over banks like First Horizon.

Mortgage warehouse lending was weak seasonally (down more than 6% qoq), but up about 38% yoy. This is more of a “coiled spring” sort of business for First Horizon. Unlike many banks, First Horizon didn’t bail out of this business when activity plunged on higher rates, and if or when rates move back below 6% for 30-year mortgages, a refi wave should drive significant growth and revenue/profits from this line.

Looking at rates, First Horizon benefited from a 25bp qoq decline in interest-bearing deposit costs, but spot rates have more or less flattened out and deposit competition could well pick up in the summer. At the same time, lending markets are competitive enough that there’s not much pricing power here without compromising credit quality, particularly with an influx of regional/super-regional banks into the Southeast lending market.

The sustainability of First Horizon’s growth really rests on balance sheet growth now. Management has caught up on balance sheet restructuring (deposit growth) in the wake of the failed Toronto-Dominion (TD) deal, and First Horizon enjoys a strong core deposit franchise in markets like Tennessee and North Carolina, but competition is picking up and a 95% loan/deposit ratio doesn’t leave a lot of wiggle room.

As I said above, a recovery in regional CRE activity would be a definite positive for the bank, as would a stronger recovery in small/mid-sized business lending. First Horizon has a strong franchise here in its core market with good customer relationships, but there’s not a lot that the bank can do if business uncertainty remains high and/or the Fed hikes rates in response to inflationary pressures.

The Outlook

Many of these macro challenges are largely outside of management’s direct control – while the bank can gain share in C&I lending and/or deposits in its core markets, overall demand is outside of their control. I do think increased M&A activity in the region should help the bank on the margin (hiring away loan officers and/or luring away customers), and I also expect a “slow and steady” effort to expand branches in already-strong markets like North Carolina and Tennessee.

M&A remains a key unknown and a potential source of volatility. A harshly negative reaction from the Street has led management to back away from prior comments about a willingness to look for deals, but I still think management would consider a tuck-in deal and/or branch acquisitions if the right opportunity emerged. At the same time, I think the bank’s strong fee-generating businesses (including bond trading and leasing) and strong lending/deposit franchise make it a credible M&A target for a super-regional looking to accelerate their Southeastern growth ambitions.

As far as modeling goes, good expense leverage remains a definite positive, and I do think loan growth will pick up provided that the U.S. economy isn’t dragged down by persistently higher energy prices. I’m a little above the Street for FY’26 EPS at $2.18 (vs. $2.14) but I expect that Street number will head higher as sell-side analysts publish updated models after the Q1 report.

I’m looking for core earnings growth of around 7% over the next five years and long-term growth closer to 5%. That supports a fair value of around $26/share. I get a similar number from my ROTCE-driven P/TBV and P/E approaches, with an actual range of $25.80 from ROTCE (assuming a 15% ROTCE) to $26.20 using a 12x multiple on my ’26 EPS estimate.

The Bottom Line

It’s certainly possible that the first quarter earnings reporting cycle will drive a broader set of model and fair value revisions, but at this point First Horizon looks like a relative bargain in the space. Upside of around 10% isn’t superb, but a lot of regional banks appear to offer even less, and many of the would-be bargains have operational issues (like First Citizens (FCNCA) and ongoing weakness in the VC market).

With a good management team, strong core franchises in attractive markets, and M&A optionality, not to mention a decent valuation, I think First Horizon remains a name worth considering for investors who still want exposure to regional banking.
